Journal.Title: Climacteric : the journal of the International Menopause Society

Article.ArticleTitle: Body composition and self-perception of age and health in resistance-trained postmenopausal women.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study investigated the associations among subjective age, age perception ratio (APR), self-rated health, happiness score and body composition in postmenopausal women.

A total of 46 postmenopausal women aged 50-69 years participated, divided into a resistance training group (RTG; n = 24; age 58.2 ± 5.1 years, ≥7 years training) and an untrained group (UNG; n = 22; age 58.5 ± 4.1 years). The APR, self-rated health and happiness were assessed and compared. Body composition variables included the waist-to-hip ratio, waist-to-height ratio, body fat percentage and absolute fat mass. Statistical analyses included t-tests, correlations and multiple regression to predict APR.

Women in the RTG perceived themselves as 27.1 ± 11.3% younger than their actual age, significantly more than the 10.6 ± 17.8% in the UNG (p = 0.001). Self-rated health was higher in the RTG (4.1 ± 0.7) than in the UNG (3.1 ± 1.0; p < 0.001), with no group differences in happiness (p = 0.799). The APR correlated with self-rated health (r = 0.689; p < 0.001) and happiness (r = 0.435; p = 0.003). Regression showed that self-rated health, happiness and waist-to-hip ratio predicted 56.7% of APR variance (R2 = 0.567; p < 0.001). The RTG also had better body composition values than the UNG.
Postmenopausal women engaged in long-term resistance training showed a higher APR and self-rated health, likely associated with improved body composition and younger self-perception of age.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Graduate Program in Physical Education, Catholic University of Brasília, Taguatinga, Brazil.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: This study looked at how postmenopausal women feel about their age and health, and whether doing regular resistance training affects these feelings. A total of 46 women aged between 50 and 69 years took part. Twenty-four of them had been doing resistance training for at least 7 years, while the other 22 did not exercise regularly.The women who exercised regularly felt, on average, 27% younger than their actual age, while those who did not exercise felt only 10% younger. The women in the exercise group also rated their health higher than those who did not train. However, there was no difference between the two groups when it came to happiness scores.Feeling younger was closely linked to better self-rated health and greater happiness. The study also found that self-rated health, happiness and waist-to-hip ratio together influenced how young women felt compared to their real age.In summary, postmenopausal women who have practiced resistance training for several years tend to feel younger and healthier. These benefits may be related to improvements in body composition, such as lower body fat and healthier waist measurements.
